intro: Anthropic's record-shattering $1.5 billion copyright settlement is dominating today's AI news cycle, but it's far from the only legal and political drama unfolding — Sony is going after AI music generator Udio over 30,000 songs, Trump's latest AI czar has already quit after just three months, and the UK is learning that its grand AI ambitions may literally run out of water. It's a day that underscores just how messy the real-world collisions between AI and, well, *everything* — copyright law, government leadership, natural resources, newsrooms — are getting.

stories.title: v2.1.216
stories.summary: Version 2.1.216 of Claude Code adds a new `sandbox.filesystem.disabled` setting that allows users to skip filesystem isolation while maintaining network egress control. It fixes three bugs: a performance issue in long sessions where message normalization grew quadratically causing multi-second stalls, a problem where auto mode denied commands with HTTP 401 errors after OAuth token expiration or rotation mid-session, and a bug where AskUserQuestion told Claude to continue even when the user asked it to wait or explain first.
stories.opinion: This is a solid maintenance release with meaningful improvements. The quadratic message normalization fix is particularly important as it addresses a real usability pain point for long sessions. The OAuth token rotation fix and the AskUserQuestion behavior correction show attention to edge cases that affect real-world usage. The new sandbox filesystem setting adds useful flexibility for users who need it. Overall, these are the kinds of targeted fixes and refinements that make a tool more relia…

stories.title: Man of his word: Pope Leo speeches declared human-authored by Australian AI detection tool
stories.summary: Pope Leo XIV's collection of speeches and writings, called 'Maps of Hope,' has been certified as human-authored by Proudly Human, an Australian AI detection company led by former chief scientist Dr. Alan Finkel. This certification comes less than two months after Pope Leo declared artificial intelligence the greatest threat to humanity.
stories.opinion: This is a fascinating intersection of religion, technology, and authenticity in the AI age. The fact that a pope's writings now need certification as human-authored underscores how deeply AI has permeated content creation and how trust in human authorship has eroded. It's both ironic and fitting that Pope Leo, having declared AI the greatest threat to humanity, would seek such verification — it lends credibility to his stance while highlighting the very problem he identified. The emergence of c…

stories.title: Nine to axe 30 jobs at the Age and SMH due to ‘extreme’ AI disruption
stories.summary: Nine Entertainment is cutting 30 newsroom jobs at the Sydney Morning Herald and the Age, with managing director of publishing Tory Maguire citing the 'extreme state of disruption' caused by AI as the reason. The cuts will be implemented through voluntary and targeted redundancies as part of what the company describes as an 'evolution' of its operations.
stories.opinion: This is a troubling but increasingly common development in the media industry, where AI is being used as justification for reducing human journalism staff. While AI disruption is real—particularly in how it affects search traffic, content aggregation, and advertising revenue—there's a risk that companies use 'AI disruption' as a convenient cover for cost-cutting that may have happened regardless. The loss of 30 journalists from two of Australia's most important mastheads represents a genuine di…

stories.title: Election voting advice from AI chatbots ‘inaccurate and unreliable’
stories.summary: Research conducted during a Hungarian election found that AI chatbots provide inaccurate, inconsistent, and unreliable voting advice. The study showed that chatbots recommended parties that were not even running, failed to mention relevant parties, and gave highly volatile answers to identical prompts. The findings raise serious concerns about the reliability of general-purpose AI systems in electoral contexts.
stories.opinion: This study highlights a genuinely important issue. General-purpose AI chatbots are not designed to serve as voting advice tools, and their use in this context is problematic. The volatility of responses to identical prompts underscores fundamental limitations in how these systems process and present political information. This should serve as a clear warning to voters not to rely on AI chatbots for electoral guidance, and to tech companies to implement stronger guardrails around election-relate…

stories.title: Not enough water for UK’s datacentre plans, trade body says
stories.summary: The UK water industry has warned that the country will not have enough water to support the government's planned expansion of datacentres for AI growth. Datacentres require large amounts of water for cooling servers, and the trade body has criticized the government's failure to address these cooling demands, calling the AI growth plans 'fatally flawed.' Water is consumed both directly through cooling systems and indirectly through high electrical power usage.
stories.opinion: This highlights a critical and often overlooked constraint on the rapid expansion of AI infrastructure: physical resource limitations. While much of the discourse around AI growth focuses on chips, energy, and investment, water scarcity is an equally fundamental bottleneck, especially in regions like the UK that already face water stress. The water industry's blunt criticism suggests a serious planning gap in the government's AI strategy. Sustainable datacentre expansion will require innovative…

stories.title: Anthropic’s landmark $1.5B copyright settlement is approved
stories.summary: A federal judge gave final approval to Anthropic's $1.5 billion class action copyright settlement with authors and book publishers. The settlement, believed to be the largest in U.S. copyright law history, will pay $3,000 per work across an estimated 500,000 works. The case originated from Judge William Alsup's ruling that while training AI on copyrighted text constitutes fair use, Anthropic illegally obtained books by downloading them from pirate sites like Library Genesis. Anthropic settled to avoid trial on the piracy question. The ruling does not set binding precedent since it was a single district court decision that won't be appealed, leaving the broader legal question of using copyrighted works for AI training unresolved as similar lawsuits continue against Google, Meta, Midjourney, and OpenAI.
stories.opinion: This settlement represents a significant but incomplete milestone in AI copyright law. The $3,000 per work payout feels modest given the enormous value AI companies extract from these works, and it's understandable that many authors don't view this as a win. The most consequential aspect is Judge Alsup's fair use ruling for AI training, which, while not binding precedent, sends a strong signal to the industry. Anthropic's strategic decision to settle rather than risk an appeals court ruling tha…

stories.title: Trump’s latest AI czar has already resigned
stories.summary: Chris Fall, the director of the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI), has resigned after just three months in the role, continuing a pattern of rapid turnover in Trump's AI leadership. His predecessor Collin Burns lasted less than a week before being pushed out due to his prior work at Anthropic. Former AI czar David Sacks stepped down in March. The article notes CAISI was notably excluded from a new White House AI safety program called 'Gold Eagle,' and that Google DeepMind's CEO has called for an independent industry-run standards body to handle the same mission CAISI was created for. The resignation comes amid controversy over the Commerce Department's temporary ban on Anthropic's models, debates over banning Chinese open models like Moonshot's Kimi, and CAISI's lack of transparency about its AI evaluation processes.
stories.opinion: This article paints a concerning picture of dysfunction and instability in U.S. AI governance at a critical time. The revolving door of AI czars—three departures in roughly four months—suggests either the role is poorly defined, politically toxic, or both. The fact that CAISI was excluded from the new 'Gold Eagle' oversight program while industry leaders like Hassabis are proposing alternative regulatory bodies suggests the agency is being sidelined or may lack institutional support. The report…

stories.title: Here are the 30,000 songs Sony is suing Udio’s AI music generator over
stories.summary: Sony Music Entertainment has filed a new lawsuit against AI music generator Udio over the alleged copyright infringement of more than 30,000 songs, including tracks by Elvis Presley, Beyoncé, and Harry Styles. Sony claims this list represents only a small portion of its infringed works, identified through audio fingerprinting of Udio's training data obtained during discovery from a 2024 lawsuit. A judge had previously rejected Sony's attempt to expand the original 2024 case (filed jointly with UMG and Warner) beyond 333 works, so Sony filed this as a separate suit. Notably, UMG and Warner Music Group have since settled with Udio and are now partnering with the company.
stories.opinion: This lawsuit highlights the intensifying legal battle over AI companies using copyrighted music to train their models without permission or compensation. Sony's decision to file a separate suit after being blocked from expanding the original case shows aggressive legal strategy. The fact that UMG and Warner settled and are now partnering with Udio is particularly telling — it suggests the industry may be bifurcating between those who see AI music generators as existential threats and those who…

tweet_headlines.headline: Microsoft open-sourced __SkillOpt__, a framework for self-evolving agent skills — train them with epochs, batch sizes, and validation gates like a neural network.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
MICROSOFT JUST OPEN-SOURCED SELF-EVOLVING AGENT SKILLS.

it's called skillopt. 

skills that improve themselves the same way you train an ai model.

no more guessing whether your prompt tweaks helped. a base model runs the task, an optimizer evaluates the output and rewrites the https://t.co/b4mS7u30F2

tweet_headlines.headline: Costco's CEO reveals the Kirkland test: if a private-label product merely matches the national brand, __he sends it back__ and asks how they can exceed it.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Costco's CEO reveals the Kirkland test that makes private label feel unfair:

A buyer brought him a bacon, egg, and cheese sandwich that matched the national brand.

He sent it back.

"How can we exceed the national brand on the bacon involved in that?"

tweet_headlines.headline: Andrew Ng says what once took a team weeks to build — Pong — can now be __built in minutes with AI__. 26-minute interview worth your time.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Google Brain co-founder and Stanford professor Andrew Ng:

"One of the earliest video games in computer history was Pong, and that had taken the team weeks to build. But now, thanks to AI, you can build something like this in minutes."
